title: The Cleansing of the Temple
subtitle: Jesus drives the sellers from His Father's house, and zeal for it consumes Him

stillness: Be still before the door of the temple.
Let the noise of the marketplace fall silent in you.
Wait; the Lord is about to enter His house.
gospel: And the pasch of the Jews was at hand,
and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
And He found in the temple them that sold
And when He had made, as it were, a scourge of little cords,
He drove them all out of the temple,
the sheep also and the oxen;
and the money of the changers He poured out,
And to them that sold doves He said:
and make not the house of My Father a house of traffic.
And His disciples remembered that it was written:
The zeal of thy house hath eaten Me up.
behold: He has gone up to Jerusalem for the pasch, with His disciples.
He enters the temple — not the inner house of the Lord,
but that court where men prayed and the doctors taught,
and where the things offered in the temple were being sold.
The priests, out of greed, by many devices impoverished the people;
they set up men to sell there, and money-changers to lend under pledge,
so that even the poor traveller had no excuse to leave his offering unmade.
He makes, as it were, a scourge of little cords,
and drives them all out — sellers and changers, the sheep and the oxen.
He pours out their coins and scatters them in every direction.
He overturns their tables, the vessels of their pennies, and casts them down.
To those selling doves He says: Take these things hence;
make not, by your work, My Father's house a house of trade and bargaining.
Mark that the scourge is of cords:
for, as Augustine says, from our own sins He takes the matter with which He punishes us,
and the drawing-out of sin, when sin is added to sin, these are the very cords.
consider: Daily, Alcuin says, God enters His Church in spirit, and watches how each one lives.
Let us beware, then, lest in the Church of God we give ourselves to idle tales, to laughter, to hatreds, to greed,
lest He come unforeseen and scourge us, and cast us out of His Church.
The sellers in the Church, says Augustine, are they who seek their own, not the things of Jesus Christ;
they hold everything for sale, because they wish to be paid.
By the sheep, that clothe with their fleece, are signified deeds of innocence and piety —
sold, when they are done under hypocrisy for human praise:
for the hypocrite has a wolf's mind beneath the sheep's skin.
By the oxen that plough are meant the preachers of heavenly doctrine,
whom they sell who preach not for the love of God, but for worldly gain.
They sell doves who give the grace of the Holy Spirit not freely, but for a price —
if not for money, then for the favour of the crowd.
They lend coin in the Church who serve, not heaven, but earthly things, and seek their own, not Jesus'.
All these Christ cast out of the temple, signifying that they are shut out from the temple of God's holy glory.
Let those, then, who would not be carried off when the Lord comes, carry these things out of their own acts;
let them cease from the dealings of avarice and simony.
For now they are scourged with the cords of their sins, unto correction;
but if uncorrected, in the end they shall be bound, unto damnation.
prayer: Brethren, let every Christian, in the members of Christ, be eaten up with zeal for the house of God.
Who is eaten up with zeal for the house of God?
He who, whatever he sees there gone wrong, strives to have it corrected, longs to see it amended, and is not at rest.
If you cannot amend it, bear it, groan, sigh, endure the chaff.
You see a brother running to the theatre? Forbid him, warn him, grieve over him — so the zeal of the Lord's house eats you up.
You see others running, and willing to be drunk? Forbid whom you can, hold whom you can, frighten whom you can.
But if you are cold and withered, looking to yourself alone,
and say in your heart: What is it to me to care for the sins of others? My own soul is enough, that I keep it whole for God —
does there not come to your mind that servant who hid his talent, and would not put it out?
Was he not accused, not because he lost it, but because he kept it without gain?
So then hear, my brethren, that you be not at rest:
cease not to gain souls for Christ, for you yourselves were gained by Christ.

latinJewel.english: The zeal of thy house hath eaten me up.
